narrative: As a white male applicant with a high family income bracket and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in some positive ways, but the admissions model focused heavily on predictors of bar exam success. The model assessed your overall application and predicted a 93.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application for admission was rejected—schools use this to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance signal stood out strongly to the model, as it has a track record of linking later-year struggles to bar exam challenges. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again well below the average, reinforcing a pattern of early underperformance compared to most applicants who rank higher from the start. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the dataset average of about 37, added further weight toward the failure prediction, though its impact was smaller than your deciles. These three academic metrics—especially the Year 3 decile—dominated the model's reasoning, outweighing strengths like your full-time status, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often have stronger decile rankings and higher LSATs, your metrics painted a riskier picture for bar passage. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 is somewhat below average and your high family income didn't sway things much, it was those core academic signals that tipped the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a supportive background—many overcome similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and reapplying with evidence of improvement, perhaps targeting schools with more holistic reviews or bar prep support programs. With targeted effort on these areas, you can shift future predictions in your favor.
